patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] nfsd: release OPEN-decoded posix ACLs via op_release

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] nfsd: release OPEN-decoded posix ACLs via op_release

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory leak in the NFS server by releasing posix ACLs allocated during OPEN compound processing. It introduces a new op_release function for OP_OPEN and ensures that resources are properly cleaned up on both the normal path and the v4.0 replay path.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Suggested moving the ->op_release() call to a more obvious and correct place in nfsd4_encode_operation().
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 01 Jun 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_decode_createhow4() calls nfsd4_decode_fattr4(), which allocates
> refcounted struct posix_acl objects via posix_acl_alloc() and stores
> them in open->op_pacl and open->op_dpacl. These pointers must be
> released once the OPEN compound finishes.
> 
> When nfsd4_decode_open_claim4() returns a non-seqid-mutating error,
> the dispatcher short-circuits before op_func runs:
> 
>     nfsd4_proc_compound()
>       opdesc->op_func == nfsd4_open_omfg
>         if (!seqid_mutating_err(ntohl(op->status)))
>             return op->status;   /* nfsd4_open() never runs */
>       opdesc->op_release(&op->u)  /* must still release op_pacl/op_dpacl */
> 
> Before this change OP_OPEN had no .op_release in nfsd4_ops[], and the
> release pair lived inside nfsd4_open() at its out_err: label. On the
> short-circuit path nfsd4_open() is never invoked, so both posix_acl
> refs leak on every malformed OPEN compound that carries valid POSIX
> ACL createhow4 attributes.
> 
> Add nfsd4_open_release() and wire it as .op_release for OP_OPEN.
> posix_acl_release() is NULL-safe, so the single release site covers
> both the normal path and the nfsd4_open_omfg short-circuit. Remove
> the matching posix_acl_release() pair from nfsd4_open()'s out_err:
> label to avoid double-releasing.
> 
> The compound loop has two encoding branches: nfsd4_encode_operation()
> for normal ops, and nfsd4_encode_replay() for v4.0 replayed ops.
> op_release was only called from nfsd4_encode_operation(), so resources
> attached to op->u leak on the replay path. Add an op_release call to
> the replay branch as well to ensure cleanup on every path.
> 
> Fixes: 5fc51dfc2eb1 ("NFSD: Add support for XDR decoding POSIX draft ACLs")
> Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
> Reviewed-by: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
> Changes in v2:
> - Ensure that op_release is called in the v4.0 replay case as well
> - Link to v1: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260531-nfsd-testing-v1-0-7bfa481b0540@kernel.org
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c | 12 ++++++++++--
>  1 file changed, 10 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> index 017474cd63b5..51998d7885ae 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4proc.c
> @@ -681,8 +681,6 @@ nfsd4_open(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
>  	nfsd4_cleanup_open_state(cstate, open);
>  	nfsd4_bump_seqid(cstate, status);
>  out_err:
> -	posix_acl_release(open->op_dpacl);
> -	posix_acl_release(open->op_pacl);
>  	return status;
>  }
>  
> @@ -704,6 +702,13 @@ static __be32 nfsd4_open_omfg(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nfsd4_compound_stat
>  	return nfsd4_open(rqstp, cstate, &op->u);
>  }
>  
> +static void
> +nfsd4_open_release(union nfsd4_op_u *u)
> +{
> +	posix_acl_release(u->open.op_dpacl);
> +	posix_acl_release(u->open.op_pacl);
> +}
> +
>  /*
>   * filehandle-manipulating ops.
>   */
> @@ -3214,6 +3219,8 @@ nfsd4_proc_compound(struct svc_rqst *rqstp)
>  			op->replay = &cstate->replay_owner->so_replay;
>  			nfsd4_encode_replay(resp->xdr, op);
>  			status = op->status = op->replay->rp_status;
> +			if (op->opdesc->op_release)
> +				op->opdesc->op_release(&op->u);
>  		} else {
>  			nfsd4_encode_operation(resp, op);
>  			status = op->status;

I think this patch is good, but I think it would be even better if the
->op_release() call were moved out of nfsd4_encode_operation() and
places after this if-else.  Then there would be only one call-site in a
fairly obviously-correct place.
But:
  Reviewed-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>
for if you just want to stick with this version.

Thanks,
NeilBrown


> @@ -3718,6 +3725,7 @@ static const struct nfsd4_operation nfsd4_ops[] = {
>  	},
>  	[OP_OPEN] = {
>  		.op_func = nfsd4_open,
> +		.op_release = nfsd4_open_release,
>  		.op_flags = OP_HANDLES_WRONGSEC | OP_MODIFIES_SOMETHING,
>  		.op_name = "OP_OPEN",
>  		.op_rsize_bop = nfsd4_open_rsize,
> 
> ---
> base-commit: 6c0004650ba248a12937ada16f9ba961b35ce2b5
> change-id: 20260531-nfsd-testing-9122bf51ce95
> 
> Best regards,
> -- 
> Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> 
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/6] nfsd: medium-severity bugfixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/6] nfsd: size fh_verify server sockaddr slot by xpt_locallen
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon's tracepoint handling by sizing the server sockaddr slot correctly when copying from xpt_local. The issue was that the slot was sized by xpt_remotelen, but filled from xpt_local using xpt_locallen, leading to potential buffer overflows or underflows. The fix is to size the server slot with xpt_locallen, matching the copy length. This patch only affects the nfsd_fh_verify and nfsd_fh_verify_err tracepoints, which are now consistent with other NFSD tracepoints that record the server address.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/6] nfsd: release path refs on follow_down() error
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory leak in the NFS daemon by releasing path references when follow_down() fails. The issue occurs because the error handling for follow_down() does not properly release the mntget() and dget() references taken on entry, leading to increased reference counts that can block unmounts and pin dentries against the shrinker. The patch adds a call to path_put(&path) before exiting the error handling branch in nfsd_cross_mnt(), ensuring that these references are properly released even when follow_down() fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/6] nfsd: fix nfsd_file leak on inter-server COPY setup failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory leak in the NFS server (nfsd) caused by failed inter-server COPY operations. When nfsd4_setup_inter_ssc() fails, it directly returns an error code without releasing the reference to the nf_dst object, leading to a file leak. The fix involves setting the status and jumping to the out label instead of returning directly, ensuring that the reference is properly released.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/6] nfsd: fix dentry ref leak on V4ROOT export filehandle lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory leak in the NFSv3 filehandle handling code. When a V4ROOT export's fsid is targeted, the dentry reference is not properly released, leading to a memory leak on every request. The patch adds a call to dput(dentry) when NFSEXP_V4ROOT is detected, ensuring that the reference count is correctly updated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/6] nfsd: release OPEN-decoded posix ACLs via op_release
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a double-put bug in the NFS server, where POSIX ACLs allocated for OPEN compounds were not being properly released. The fix involves adding an op_release function to the OP_OPEN operation and updating the nfsd4_open_omfg function to release the ACLs on the replay path.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified a gap in the original fix that would leak POSIX ACL references on v4.0 replay paths and suggested an additional change to release the ACLs on this path.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the gap and agreed to make the additional change.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sun, 2026-05-31 at 11:54 -0400, Chuck Lever wrote:
> On Sun, May 31, 2026, at 8:07 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
> 
> > Remove the matching posix_acl_release() pair from nfsd4_open()'s
> > out_err: label: the compound dispatcher calls op_release
> > unconditionally after every op, [...]
> 
> The double-free fix is right, but op_release is not called
> unconditionally after every op, and that gap leaks the ACLs on the
> v4.0 replay path.
> 
> op_release runs only at the release: label inside
> nfsd4_encode_operation().  The compound loop skips that encoder on
> a replay:
> 
>         if (op->status == nfserr_replay_me) {
>                 nfsd4_encode_replay(resp->xdr, op);     /* no op_release */
>                 ...
>         } else {
>                 nfsd4_encode_operation(resp, op);       /* op_release here */
>         }
> 
> So every ACL-bearing v4.0 OPEN retransmit leaks two posix_acl refs.
> 
> Please release op->u on the replay branch too:
> 
>         if (op->status == nfserr_replay_me) {
>                 op->replay = &cstate->replay_owner->so_replay;
>                 nfsd4_encode_replay(resp->xdr, op);
>                 status = op->status = op->replay->rp_status;
>                 if (op->opdesc->op_release)
>                         op->opdesc->op_release(&op->u);
>         }
> 
> Let's fix the "unconditionally after every op" wording too.
> 
> I've applied the other 5 in this series, so you can just resend
> this one.
> 

Good catch, will fix.

I initially had hoped that we could rely on the fact that a v4.0 client
couldn't issue an OPEN with a POSIX ACL attribute in it, but it turns
out that knfsd doesn't gate attributes by minorversion.

Maybe it should? That's a separate changeset though if so.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/6] nfsd: fix layout fence worker double-reference race
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a double-reference race condition in the NFSd layout fence worker. The issue arises when the workqueue core clears WORK_STRUCT_PENDING before invoking the callback, allowing lm_breaker_timedout() to take a duplicate reference on the layout stateid. To fix this, the patch introduces an ls_fence_inflight boolean flag that is set atomically with refcount_inc_not_zero() under ls_lock and cleared under ls_lock before nfs4_put_stid() on every exit path. This ensures that a fence worker invocation already in progress does not coalesce with a new one, preventing a memory leak of the layout stateid.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses medium-severity bugs in the NFS daemon, including memory leaks and refcounting issues. The patches fix problems in various files related to file handling, path references, and layout fence workers. Specifically, they address potential buffer overruns in tracepoints and double-reference races.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified a double-free bug in the NFS daemon where ACLs are not properly released on the v4.0 replay path. Suggested adding an extra call to op->opdesc->op_release() to fix the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Sun, 31 May 2026 08:06:57 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> These are classified as medium-severity, remote-triggerable.  Some
> memory leaks and refcounting bugs, and a potential buffer overrun in a
> tracepoint.

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/6] nfsd: size fh_verify server sockaddr slot by xpt_locallen
      commit: bbbc993f3932be9428ebf81009d0d1a4aed26d00
[2/6] nfsd: release path refs on follow_down() error
      commit: 41c9073b511fe70d5e3b03f039c409d2060de06b
[3/6] nfsd: fix nfsd_file leak on inter-server COPY setup failure
      commit: b55429cfce784ffcbaed2960d66b7c5db90ceec3
[4/6] nfsd: fix dentry ref leak on V4ROOT export filehandle lookup
      commit: 657c7e3082589c35695f77821f1dfa31bf4c6a34
[6/6] nfsd: fix layout fence worker double-reference race
      commit: 4faa69d7158a4436d340b7324aa34fa672362b5c

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the double-free issue and agreed to fix it. Mentioned that the problem is due to a gap in the op_release() call on the v4.0 replay path.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 5/6] nfsd: release OPEN-decoded posix ACLs via op_release
Re: [PATCH] vfs: missing inode operation should return a consistent error code

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 2/2] SUNRPC: Check svc pool percpu counter allocation

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/8] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru
[PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a long-standing issue in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup during a race condition between list_lru_add() and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the 'effective' memcg from the locking function and having the caller use that. This change ensures that the shrinker bit is correctly set on the cgroup that actually has the object.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acked the patch without any issues or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
> cgroup that actually has the object.
> 
> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
> parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
> shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
> 
> This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
> 
> Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
> have the caller use that.
> 
> Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
> Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> Reported-by: Sashiko
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  mm/list_lru.c | 26 +++++++++++++++-----------
>  1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 11 deletions(-)
> 

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch and suggested it doesn't need to be sent to stable, as it's a non-critical fix. Provided an inline review with comments.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:08PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
>on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
>cgroup that actually has the object.
>
>When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
>parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
>shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
>

This means we just miss to reclaim some obj, but won't crash the kernel.

>This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
>
>Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
>have the caller use that.
>
>Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
>Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
>Reported-by: Sashiko
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

So we don't want to cc stable, right?

The fix looks right, so

Reviewed-by: Wei Yang <richard.weiyang@gmail.com>

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by sharing a common implementation between the MEMCG and !MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication.

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Suggested unifying the parameter name in lock_list_lru() to improve code readability and consistency.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:12PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
>
>Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>Reviewed-by: Liam R. Howlett (Oracle) <liam@infradead.org>
>---
> mm/list_lru.c | 21 +++++++++------------
> 1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
>
>diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
>index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
>--- a/mm/list_lru.c
>+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
>@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
> #include "slab.h"
> #include "internal.h"

Hi, Johannes

One very tiny nit below.

> 
>+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)

Here we use @irq.

>+{
>+	if (irq)
>+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
>+	else
>+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
>+}
>+
> static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)

Here we use @irq_off.

Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?

Also the name in callsite and the argument annotation. Would it be cleaner
readers?

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Agreed to send a follow-up patch on top of mm-unstable to address the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 09:42:02AM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:

>> >The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.

>> > 1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
>> >
>> >diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
>> >index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
>> >--- a/mm/list_lru.c
>> >+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
>> >@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
>> > #include "slab.h"
>> > #include "internal.h"
>> 
>> Hi, Johannes
>> 
>> One very tiny nit below.
>> 
>> > 
>> >+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
>> 
>> Here we use @irq.
>> 
>> >+{
>> >+	if (irq)
>> >+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
>> >+	else
>> >+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
>> >+}
>> >+
>> > static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)
>> 
>> Here we use @irq_off.
>> 
>> Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?
>
>Yes, I think it would be nicer.
>
>Note that I inherited this - we had irq on the lock and irq_off on the
>unlock before already. I didn't want to mix even more yak shaving prep
>patches into this series.

Reasonable.

>
>Mind sending a follow-up patch on top of mm-unstable?

Thanks, I am glad to.

Since this is trivial, I would wait until everything is settle down.

Looks good to you?

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Suggested waiting until everything is settled down before sending a follow-up patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:12PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
>
>Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>Reviewed-by: Liam R. Howlett (Oracle) <liam@infradead.org>
>---
> mm/list_lru.c | 21 +++++++++------------
> 1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
>
>diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
>index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
>--- a/mm/list_lru.c
>+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
>@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
> #include "slab.h"
> #include "internal.h"

Hi, Johannes

One very tiny nit below.

> 
>+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)

Here we use @irq.

>+{
>+	if (irq)
>+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
>+	else
>+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
>+}
>+
> static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)

Here we use @irq_off.

Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?

Also the name in callsite and the argument annotation. Would it be cleaner
readers?

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me


---

>> >index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
>> >--- a/mm/list_lru.c

>> >@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
>> > #include "slab.h"
>> > #include "internal.h"
>> 
>> Hi, Johannes

>> One very tiny nit below.
>>

>> >+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)
>> 
>> Here we use @irq.
>> 
>> >+{
>> >+	if (irq)
>> >+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
>> >+	else
>> >+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
>> >+}
>> >+
>> > static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)
>> 
>> Here we use @irq_off.

>> Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?
>
>Yes, I think it would be nicer.
>
>Note that I inherited this - we had irq on the lock and irq_off on the
>unlock before already. I didn't want to mix even more yak shaving prep
>patches into this series.

Thanks, I am glad to.

Looks good to you?

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch flattens the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop to improve code readability and maintainability, making it easier for future patches to be added without cluttering the existing code.

review_comments.author: Dev Jain
review_comments.summary: Applied the Reviewed-by tag to indicate that the patch looks good from their perspective.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a folio is split across multiple nodes, and requested that the list_lru per-memcg heads be instantiated on demand when the first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup.

Reviewer SeongJae Park reported that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error, specifically for the folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() function when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset. They suggested defining the function for this case and provided a temporal fix as evidence.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch is missing a definition for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE is unset, causing an implicit function declaration error in UM mode kunit tests.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, 27 May 2026 16:45:16 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

---

I found this patch is now in mm-new and it makes UM mode kunit fails like
below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Maybe we can define the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE unset case?  I
confirmed the below attaching temporal fix works for at least kunit.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]
=== >8 ===

---

From: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date: Wed, 27 May 2026 23:58:07 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] mm/huge_mm: define memcg_alloc_deferred() for
 !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

Without this, UM mode kunit fails like below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Fix by implementing the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE unset
case.

Fixes: https://lore.kernel.org/20260527204757.2544958-10-hannes@cmpxchg.org
Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
---
 include/linux/huge_mm.h | 5 +++++
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)

diff --git a/include/linux/huge_mm.h b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
index f6c2531a27a35..055de7b8ed487 100644
--- a/include/linux/huge_mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
@@ -663,6 +663,11 @@ static inline int folio_split(struct folio *folio, unsigned int new_order,
 	return -EINVAL;
 }
 
+static inline int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
+{
+	return 0;
+}
+
 static inline void deferred_split_folio(struct folio *folio, bool partially_mapped) {}
 #define split_huge_pmd(__vma, __pmd, __address)	\
 	do { } while (0)
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the necessity of incrementing count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK) in a specific scenario, suggesting either removing it or modifying the control flow to avoid this increment
review_comments.raw_body:
Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need

count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);

here?

or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
viable order?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Usama Arif's concern that an unexpected failure of a sub-costly-order slab allocation could be missed by the event counter, explaining that such failures are rare and would already be logged in dmesg.

Author addressed a concern about the naming convention for the list_lru allocation, agreeing that 'fallback' is more correct and choosing to leave the code as is.
review_comments.raw_body:
This isn't an allocation we expect to fail with any sort of routine
that we'd need to capture it in the event counter. It would warn in
dmesg if it did. But in practice it can't happen at all, since it's a
sub-costly-order slab allocation and the allocator would loop and OOM
kill stuff until it succeeds.

---

Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
orders (until we hit 0).

So I think we can just leave it as is.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake in their testing and promised to add UM (User Mode) builds to their final compile test before resubmitting the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Whoops, thanks for the fix, SJ. I'll incorporate UM builds into my
final compile test before sending.

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested moving error handling under a shared label for consistency and potential future simplification
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks!

Nit: I think it would be better if we move the error handling under a
label to be shared with the charge failure above, but it's fine this
way, can be simplified later.

Reviewed-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Wei Yang pointed out that there are three possible failure points and some duplicate count_xxx_event/state() calls in the patch, suggesting a follow-up cleanup to address these issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
Nit: we have three possible failure point, and some duplicate
count_xxx_event/state().

Maybe we can have a followup cleanup for it.

Others, looks good. Thanks.

-- 
Wei Yang

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
SeongJae Park noted that the patch does not handle the case where a THP is being split and its child folios are still on the list_lru, which can lead to a use-after-free error when the parent folio is freed.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset, and suggested defining the function for this case.

SeongJae Park noted that without the definition of folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() for !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE, UM mode kunit fails due to an implicit function declaration error in __swap_cache_alloc(), and provided a patch to define this function.

kill stuff until it succeeds.

---

Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
orders (until we hit 0).

So I think we can just leave it as is.
